fact: November 3 is exactly 307 days into the year, which means there are only 58 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for squirrels to go absolutely nuts burying acorns - they can bury up to 10,000 acorns in a single season!
The sun sets before 5 PM in many northern cities around this date, making it feel like bedtime when you're just getting home from school.
November 3 falls smack in the middle of Scorpio season - the zodiac sign represented by a creature that glows under black light!
Maple trees are dropping their leaves like crazy right now in northern climates, and a single large maple can dump over 200,000 leaves in autumn.
Why do mornings feel so much colder around this time? The angle of Earth's tilt means the Northern Hemisphere is getting way less direct sunlight.
Deer are typically in their mating season right now in North America, which makes them act totally weird - male deer will fight each other by smashing their antlers together!
The moon often looks extra bright and crisp in early November because the cold, dry air acts like a natural magnifying glass.
November 3 is roughly halfway between the autumn equinox and winter solstice - it's like being on a seasonal seesaw!
Spiders are desperately trying to get inside houses around this date in northern climates because they know winter is coming and they'll freeze outside.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, November 3 is actually spring - flowers are blooming while the north is raking leaves!
The first frost of the year often happens around this time in many temperate regions, turning spider webs into glittery ice sculptures overnight.
